blocks.content:
Until now, each line of code in the program was executed only once.

But sometimes a program needs to repeat the same action several times.

A loop is a part of the program that repeats a block of code.

This repetition is controlled by conditions written in the code.

A loop does not repeat code randomly.

It always follows a rule that decides whether the next repetition should happen.

Let’s look at a simple idea.

Imagine a program that checks a PIN code.

The same check needs to run multiple times until something changes.

There are two common situations when the repetition should stop:

- the correct PIN code is entered
- the number of attempts reaches a limit (for example, 3)

The program repeats the same block of code again and again while these conditions are not met.

Each repetition runs the same instructions.

Before each new repetition, the program checks a condition.

If the condition is true, the loop continues.

If the condition becomes false, the loop stops.

So every loop has two key parts:

- a block of code that is repeated
- a condition that controls when the repetition stops

Important:

A loop runs while the condition is true.

True → the code runs again
False → the loop stops
